editorial.headline: Five-Run Fifth Powers New York Yankees Past Chicago
editorial.subheadline: CWS 5, NYY 10. Top of the division changes hands — or firms up.
editorial.lead_angle:
A five-run fifth inning busted the game open at Yankee Stadium, carrying the New York Yankees to a 10-5 victory over the Chicago White Sox and into sole possession of first place. The rally turned a taut, one-run contest into a rout, chasing Chicago pitching from the mound and quieting the visiting dugout. Carlos Rodón picked up the win for New York, weathering an early storm to work through the middle innings. The loss was charged to Anthony Kay, who was on the hook for the rally that erased any early Chicago momentum.

The White Sox had clawed back into the game in the third, making the score 4-3 and putting a hush over the Bronx. For a moment, the Yankees looked vulnerable. But in the bottom of the fifth, New York responded with authority. The inning was a blur of baserunners and hard contact, capped by a sharp single from José Caballero that plated two runs and stretched the lead to an insurmountable margin. After Caballero’s hit, the air went out of the White Sox comeback attempt for good.

Colson Montgomery was the lone beacon for Chicago in a pivotal loss. The shortstop accounted for nearly all the team’s offense, finishing 2-for-4 with two home runs and four runs batted in. His three-run shot in the third inning pulled the White Sox back into the game, and his solo blast in the ninth was a final, futile gesture. But his individual effort could not prevent the slide in the standings, as the Yankees used the victory to create crucial separation in the division race with summer heating up.
editorial.reason: Two division leaders, one winner — the standings implications make this the marquee matchup.
